27: tharsein — take courage, be of good cheer — an imperative of encouragement used in crisis moments; Jesus speaks it to the storm-panicked disciples and to the paralytic (Matthew 9:2) and to the hemorrhaging woman (Matthew 9:22). ego eimi — I am — the LXX divine self-disclosure formula (Exodus 3:14, Isaiah 41:4, Matthew 43:10); spoken in the darkness on the sea to terrified disciples, the formula identifies Jesus with the divine presence that crossed the Red Sea. me phobeisthe — do not fear — the standard divine reassurance formula throughout the OT (Genesis 15:1, Isaiah 41:10, Matthew 43:1).
1: en ekeinō tō kairō ēkousen Hērōdēs ho tetrarchēs tēn akoēn Iēsou — at that time Herod the tetrarch heard the report about Jesus (tetrarchēs: ruler of a quarter — Herod Antipas held the tetrarchy of Galilee and Perea under Roman appointment after his father Herod the Great's death; the title is less than "king" — Matthew 14:9 uses "king" loosely as a courtesy title) — tēn akoēn Iēsou (the report/hearing about Jesus — akoē: what is heard, the public report): Herod's hearing connects back to the disciples being sent out in Matthew 10 and the spreading fame since Matthew 9.
2: kai eipen tois paisin autou — and he said to his servants/attendants — houtos estin Iōannēs ho baptistēs (this is John the Baptist): Herod's identification is diagnostic of a guilty conscience — he sees the miraculous deeds of Jesus and interprets them through the lens of his act against John. Autos ēgerthē apo tōn nekrōn (he has risen from the dead — ēgerthē aorist passive of egeirō: the resurrection verb; Herod uses the standard resurrection vocabulary to describe his fear): kai dia touto hai dynameis energousin en autō (and therefore miraculous powers are at work in him — energousin: are working, are active — the word from which "energy" derives).
3: ho gar Hērōdēs kratēsas ton Iōannēn edēsen — for Herod had seized John and bound him (kratēsas aorist participle of krateō: to seize, take hold of forcefully; edēsen aorist of deō: to bind — the binding vocabulary of 12:29 now applied literally to John) — kai en phylakē apetheto dia Hērōdiada tēn gynaika Philippou tou adelphou autou (and placed him in prison because of Herodias, the wife of his brother Philip): Herodias had left Philip to marry Antipas; John's denunciation of this as unlawful (Matthew 14:4) was politically and dynastically dangerous.
4: elegen gar ho Iōannēs autō — for John had been saying to him (elegen imperfect: was repeatedly saying, habitually denouncing — not a single accusation but a sustained prophetic rebuke) — ouk exestin soi echein autēn (it is not lawful for you to have her — ouk exestin: the same lawfulness-formula as the Sabbath controversies of Matthew 12): John applies the same Torah-appeal to a political marriage that Jesus applies to Sabbath healing. The Levitical prohibitions (Leviticus 18:16; Matthew 20:21) forbade a man from marrying his brother's wife while the brother lived.
5: kai thelōn auton apokteinai ephobēthē ton ochlon — wanting to kill him he was afraid of the crowd (thelōn present participle: continuously wanting/desiring to kill him; ephobēthē aorist passive: was made afraid): Herod's murderous desire is restrained not by scruple but by political calculation. Hoti hōs prophētēn auton eichon (because they held him as a prophet — eichon imperfect: were holding, in their considered estimation): the crowd's recognition of John as a prophet is the same recognition that produces Herod's restraint — and that produces the crowd's recognition in Matthew 21:26.
6: genesiois de genomenos tou Hērōdou — on the birthday celebration of Herod (genesia: birthday celebration — the word can refer to the birthday itself or the festivities; birthday celebrations for rulers were known in the Hellenistic world) — ōrchēsato hē thygatēr tēs Hērōdiados en tō mesō (the daughter of Herodias danced in the midst — ōrchēsato aorist of orcheomai: to dance; dancing as entertainment for male guests was associated with lower social status; a princess dancing publicly would have been unusual and attention-seizing) — kai ēresen tō Hērōdē (and pleased Herod — areskō): the dancing produces the rash oath.
7: hothen met horkous hōmologēsen autē dounai ho ean aitēsētai — whereupon he promised with an oath to give her whatever she might ask (met horkous: with oaths — plural, sworn solemnly; hōmologēsen aorist of homologeō: to publicly declare, promise, confess — the oath-making is a formal public commitment, not a private aside): the oath binds Herod before witnesses. Ho ean aitēsētai (whatever she might ask — aitēsētai aorist subjunctive: a fully open promise): the terms are blanket and unconditional, which the next verse exploits.
8: hē de probibastheisa hypo tēs mētros autēs — but she, prompted/led forward by her mother (probibastheisa aorist passive participle of probibazō: to cause to step forward, to incite, to put up to something — the mother is the agent behind the request) — dos moi phēsin hōde epi pinaki tēn kephalēn Iōannou tou baptistou (she says: give me here on a dish the head of John the Baptist — hōde: here, immediately; epi pinaki: on a platter, on a serving dish): the gruesome specificity of the request — a severed head delivered on serving-ware at a banquet — is designed to make refusal maximally embarrassing before the guests.
9: kai lypētheis ho basileus — and being grieved, the king (lypētheis aorist passive participle of lypeō: being grieved, distressed — Herod is not indifferent; his distress is real) — dia tous horkous kai tous synanakeimenous (because of the oaths and those reclining with him — the dinner guests): the two competing pressures are the public oath (which would dishonor him if broken) and the opinion of the guests (before whom he must appear strong). Herod is trapped by his own rash promise and his need to maintain honor. Ekeleusen dothēnai (he commanded it to be given).
10: kai pempsas apekephalisen Iōannēn en tē phylakē — and sending he had John beheaded in the prison (apekephalisen aorist of apokephalzō: to behead — a compound verb; apo: away from + kephalē: head): the execution is narrated with clinical brevity. Matthew provides no scene-setting; the bare fact of John's death stands as a marker of the opposition that Jesus will face. John's fate anticipates Jesus's: both die by the political authority's hand under pressure from religious/domestic opponents.
11: kai ēnechthē hē kephalē autou epi pinaki kai edothē tō korasio — and his head was brought on a platter and given to the girl (ēnechthē aorist passive: was brought/carried; the passive voice removes the bearers from sight, focusing on the object): the head's journey from prison to platter to girl to mother is told in four passive or impersonal verbs. Kai ēnenken tē mētri autēs (and she brought it to her mother): the chain of agency runs backward — Herod → executioner → girl → mother; the mother who initiated the sequence receives the result.
12: kai proselthontes hoi mathētai autou ēran to ptōma kai ethapsan auton — and his disciples coming took the body and buried it (ptōma: corpse, fallen body — the word for a fallen/dead body, from piptō: to fall) — kai elthontes apēngilan tō Iēsou (and coming they reported to Jesus): John's disciples perform the final act of loyalty — burial — and then bring the news to Jesus. The report to Jesus closes the John narrative and opens the withdrawal/feeding pericope: Jesus receives the news of John's death and withdraws (Matthew 14:13).
13: akousas de ho Iēsous anechōrēsen ekeithen en ploiō eis erēmon topon — hearing this, Jesus withdrew in a boat to a deserted place (akousas aorist participle: having heard; anechōrēsen: withdrew — the same deliberate withdrawal verb as Matthew 12:15; erēmon topon: a deserted/solitary place — erēmos: without inhabitants, wild) — kat idian (privately, by himself): Jesus's withdrawal in response to John's death is more than grief; it is a strategic recognition that the political danger has escalated. Kai akousantes hoi ochloi ēkolouthēsan autō pezē apo tōn poleōn (the crowds, hearing, followed him on foot from the cities): the withdrawal is followed, not achieved.
14: kai exelthōn eiden polun ochlon — and going out he saw a large crowd — kai esplanchnisthē ep autois (and he had compassion on them — esplanchnisthē aorist passive of splagchnizomai: the strongest compassion-verb, from splanchnon/intestines; cf. Matthew 9:36): the compassion overrides the private grief that motivated the withdrawal. Kai etherapeuse tous arrōstous autōn (and he healed their sick ones — arrōstous: literally without strength, weakened, sick): the compassion-response is healing, not merely sympathy. The scene recalls 9:36 where compassion led to the harvest-prayer; here it produces direct ministry.
15: opsias de genomenes prosēlthon autō hoi mathētai legontes — evening coming on, the disciples came to him saying — erēmos estin ho topos kai hē hōra ēdē parēlthen (the place is deserted and the hour has already passed — parēlthen aorist: has gone by, is past; the meal-time has elapsed): the disciples' assessment is logistically accurate. Apoluson tous ochlous hina apelthontes eis tas kōmas agorasōsin heautois brōmata (send the crowds away so that going to the villages they might buy food for themselves): the disciples' solution is dispersal; they have no framework yet for what Jesus is about to do.
16: ho de Iēsous eipen autois ou chreian echousin apelthein — but Jesus said to them: they do not need to go away — dote autois hymeis phagein (you give them something to eat — hymeis: emphatic, YOU give them — the pronoun is the challenge): Jesus redirects responsibility back to the disciples. The command is absurd by any calculation of their resources (Matthew 14:17: five loaves and two fish); its point is to expose the disciples' inadequacy and transfer the solution to Jesus. The feeding that follows is not a demonstration of Jesus's power independent of the disciples but happens through them: he gives to them, they give to the crowd.
17: hoi de legousin autō ouk echomen hōde ei mē pente artous kai duo ichthyas — they say to him: we have here only five loaves and two fish — the ei mē (except/only) clause marks the minimum: five loaves, two fish is subsistence for a handful of people, not thousands. The specification of the supplies before the miracle is a standard feature of miracle accounts that establishes the baseline against which the abundance will be measured. Hōde (here, at this location) further limits even this small supply to what is immediately at hand.
18: ho de eipen pherete moi hōde autous — he said: bring them here to me — the command pherete moi (bring to me) is the pivot: the disciples' inadequate resources become the material for Jesus's action when placed in his hands. The transfer of the loaves to Jesus precedes the multiplication; the miracle happens not by Jesus creating food independently but by transforming what is given to him. The pattern anticipates the disciples' role in distribution: they give to him; he multiplies; they distribute.
19: kai keleusas tous ochlous anapesein epi tou chortou — and commanding the crowds to recline on the grass (anapesein: to recline, the posture of reclining for a meal) — labōn tous pente artous kai tous duo ichthyas anablepas eis ton ouranon eulogēsen (taking the five loaves and the two fish, looking up to heaven, he blessed — anablepas eis ton ouranon: looking up to heaven, the prayer posture; eulogēsen aorist of eulogeō: to bless, to speak blessing over — the Jewish berakah form: "Blessed are you, Lord our God, king of the universe, who brings forth bread from the earth"): kai klesas edōken tois mathētais tous artous (and breaking he gave the loaves to the disciples — klesas: having broken; the eucharistic language anticipates Matthew 26:26).
20: kai ephagon pantes kai echortasthēsan — and they all ate and were satisfied (echortasthēsan aorist passive of chortazō: to be filled, to be satisfied — the word used in the fifth Beatitude, Matthew 5:6, for those "hungry and thirsting" for righteousness who will be filled; the same root as the animal-feeding satisfaction the Lord provides in Psalms 147:9) — kai ēran to perisseuon tōn klasmatōn dōdeka kophinous plēreis (and they gathered the surplus of the fragments, twelve baskets full — kophinos: a wicker basket, specifically a Jewish type of basket; twelve baskets correspond to the twelve disciples, or symbolically to the twelve tribes).
21: hoi de esthiontes ēsan andres hōsei pentakischilioi — those eating were about five thousand men (andres: men, adult males — specifically counted; hōsei: approximately) — chōris gynaikōn kai paidiōn (besides women and children): Matthew adds this qualifier, absent from Mark; the total crowd is therefore substantially more than five thousand. The counting of men (andres) follows the military census custom; the surplus population (women and children) are additional. This specification increases the miraculous scale beyond what any simple estimate would yield.
22: kai eutheōs ēnankasen tous mathētas embēnai eis to ploion — and immediately he compelled the disciples to get into the boat (ēnankasen aorist of enankazō: to compel, force — a strong word: not a suggestion or invitation but a compelling, as if the disciples needed to be moved with force) — kai proagein auton eis to peran (to go on ahead of him to the other side): the disciples' departure before the dismissal of the crowds is forced by Jesus — perhaps because they would have wanted to stay with him. Heōs hou apolysei tous ochlous (while he dismissed the crowds — apolysei future: will dismiss; he completes the crowd-dismissal alone).
23: kai apolusas tous ochlous anebē eis to oros kat idian proseuxasthai — dismissing the crowds he went up on the mountain privately to pray (anebē eis to oros: went up on/into the mountain — the mountain in Matthew is frequently a locus of revelation and prayer: Matthew 5:1; Matthew 17:1; Matthew 28:16; kat idian: privately, by himself; proseuxasthai aorist infinitive of proseuchomai: to pray) — opsias de genomenes monos ēn ekei (evening coming, he was there alone): the evening is the second evening of Matthew 14:15 (first evening = meal-time; second evening = nightfall). Jesus is alone on the mountain in prayer while the disciples battle the sea.
24: to de ploion ēdē stadious pollous apo tēs gēs apeichen — the boat was already many stades from land (stadious: a stade = ~185 meters; "many stades" = a significant distance from shore) — basanizomenon hypo tōn kymatōn (being tormented by the waves — basanizomenon present passive participle of basanizō: to torment, put to torture, originally of testing metals with a touchstone; used for the extreme distress of being battered by waves) — ēn gar enantios ho anemos (for the wind was contrary — enantios: against, opposing, adverse): the boat makes no progress against an opposing wind while Jesus is alone on the mountain and the disciples are alone on the sea.
25: tetartē de phylakē tēs nyktos ēlthen pros autous peripatōn epi tēn thalassan — in the fourth watch of the night he came to them walking on the sea (tetartē phylakē: the fourth watch — by Roman military reckoning, 3-6 AM; the darkest, most exhausted hour of the night) — peripatōn epi tēn thalassan (walking on the sea — peripatōn present active participle: actively walking, in motion): the divine act of walking on water echoes Job 9:8 LXX (God "walks on the sea as on firm ground") and Psalms 77:19 (God's way through the sea). The OT background makes the action a theophanic sign.
26: hoi de mathētai idontes auton epi tēs thalassēs peripatounta etarachthēsan — the disciples seeing him walking on the sea were troubled (etarachthēsan aorist passive of tarassō: to be shaken, disturbed, troubled — the strongest word for inner agitation) — legontes hoti phantasma estin kai apo tou phobou ekraxan (saying it is a ghost, and from fear they cried out — phantasma: a phantom, apparition — the disciples' terror interprets Jesus's approach as a ghost): the cry from fear (apo tou phobou) precedes Jesus's word of reassurance. Fear is the disciples' first response to theophanic presence before recognition.
28: apokritheis de autō ho Petros eipen kyrie ei sy ei — Peter answering him said: Lord, if it is you (ei sy ei: if you are the one — the conditional is not doubt but verification; Peter wants confirmation before the request) — keleuson me elthein pros se epi ta hydata (command me to come to you on the waters — keleuson aorist imperative: command; pros se: toward you, not just "across the water" but specifically to Jesus): Peter's request is not a demonstration of faith-testing but an act of faith in Jesus's capacity to enable walking. The request positions the miracle as coming from Jesus's command, not Peter's initiative.
29: ho de eipen elthet kai katabalōn apo tou ploiou ho Petros periepatēsen epi ta hydata — he said: come; and getting down from the boat Peter walked on the water (elthe aorist imperative: come; katabalōn aorist participle of katabainō: having stepped down; periepatēsen aorist: walked) — kai ēlthen pros ton Iēsoun (and came toward Jesus): the single verb periepatēsen is the action; the coming-toward-Jesus is the goal. Peter's walking on the water is real and brief — the miracle happens and is then interrupted by a change of focus.
30: blepōn de ton anemon edeiiliasen — but seeing the wind he was afraid (blepōn present participle: seeing — the problem is the ongoing looking at the wind, not a glance; edeiliaseen aorist of deiliaō: to be cowardly, to be afraid) — kai arxamenos katapontizesthai (and beginning to sink — katapontizesthai: to go down under water, to be engulfed by the sea — a compound of kata: down and pontos: the deep sea): ekraxen legōn kyrie sōson me (he cried out saying: Lord, save me! — sōson aorist imperative of sōzō: the salvation word used for healing; Peter's cry invokes both physical rescue and the full weight of sōzō).
31: eutheōs de ho Iēsous ekteinas tēn cheira epelabeto autou — immediately Jesus stretching out his hand took hold of him (eutheōs: immediately — the same immediacy-word that marked shallow enthusiasm in Matthew 13:5,20; here it marks divine rescue speed; ekteinas tēn cheira: stretching out his hand — the reaching-gesture used in 8:3 for healing the leper and 12:49 for identifying the disciples as family) — oligopiste (you of little faith — oligopiste: a compound coined by Matthew, appearing in Matthew 6:30; Matthew 8:26; Matthew 16:8; Matthew 17:20: oligos small/little + pistis faith). Eis ti edistasas (why did you waver/doubt — distasas aorist of distazō: to be divided, to stand in two directions, to waver).
32: kai anabantōn autōn eis to ploion ekopasen ho anemos — and when they got into the boat the wind ceased (anabantōn autōn genitive absolute: they having gone up into the boat; ekopasen aorist of kopazō: to grow weary, to cease, to abate): the same sequence as the storm-calming of 8:26 where the rebuke of the wind produced a great calm. Here the wind stops at the moment of boarding without an explicit rebuke. The cessation is simultaneous with, but not structurally caused by, the boarding — the storm's end marks the completion of the theophanic episode.
33: hoi de en tō ploiō prosekynēsan autō legontes — those in the boat worshipped him saying (prosekynēsan aorist of proskyneō: to worship, to bow in reverence — the same word used in Matthew 2:2,11 for the Magi's worship and in Matthew 28:9,17 for post-resurrection worship; distinguished from the "marveling" of the crowd in 8:27 after the storm) — alēthōs theou huios ei (truly you are the Son of God — alēthōs: truly, genuinely — an emphatic adverb of affirmation; theou huios: Son of God with the genitive): this is the first corporate confession of Jesus as Son of God in Matthew; it answers the demons' correct identification (Matthew 8:29) with genuine worship.
34: kai diaperasantes ēlthon epi tēn gēn Gennēsaret — crossing over they came to the land of Gennesaret (diaperasantes aorist participle of diaperaō: having crossed over, completed the sea-crossing) — Gennesaret is the fertile plain on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee, south of Capernaum. The geographical specificity grounds the ensuing healing summary (Matthew 14:35-36) in a specific locale, where Jesus's reputation is sufficient for the population to mobilize before he has even disembarked.
35: kai epignontes auton hoi andres tou topou ekeinou apesteilan eis holēn tēn perikhōron ekeinēn — recognizing him, the men of that place sent into all the surrounding region (epignontes aorist participle of epiginōskō: having recognized — the intensive recognition-verb) — kai prosēnegkan autō pantas tous kakōs echontas (and they brought to him all those who were ill — pantas: all; kakōs echontas: those having it badly, those ill): the regional mobilization to bring the sick to Jesus is the practical outworking of the recognition. Faith in his healing capacity is demonstrated by logistical effort before any word is spoken.
36: kai parekaloun auton hina monon apsōntai tou kraspedou tou himatiou autou — and they were beseeching him to let them just touch the fringe of his garment (parekaloun imperfect: were continuously beseeching; apsōntai aorist subjunctive: to touch once; kraspedon: the fringe/tassel of the garment, the tassels required by Numbers 15:38-39 — the same word as in 9:20 for the hemorrhaging woman) — kai hosoi ēpsanto diesōthēsan (and as many as touched were completely saved/healed — diesōthēsan aorist passive of diasōzō: to save/heal completely, the compound intensifying the totality): the report is collective; individuals are healed as the crowds make contact with the fringe.
